text: I am sharing a sigmoid colectomy for a smoldering diverticulitis in a patient who had never prior had a colonoscopy.
Symptoms have been really persistent for about one year. The last known attack was about six months earlier, but she just never really felt better after that attack.
And CAT scans that she had had intermittently over that amount of time always showed persistent diverticular disease.
And at one time she had an intramural abscess. Because of her symptoms and never really being able to get things under control, she didn't have a colonoscopy and opted for surgery.
She had lost close to sixty pounds during this last year.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection despite the adherence of the phlegmon to the lateral sidewall, utilizing the adherence as a natural retractor during the sigmoid colectomy.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and continues with medial dissection, focusing on the vascular structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the proximal sigmoid colon, identifying the location of the disease. The splenic flexure is taken down to ensure adequate length for the procedure.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), while evaluating whether to take the IMV during the procedure.
The surgeon improves exposure to visualize the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the ligament of Treitz, following the mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, extending laterally to facilitate the mobilization of the splenic flexure, carefully separating tissue layers to achieve adequate exposure.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization and mobilization of the splenic flexure, preparing to address potential mesenteric tension before deciding whether to take the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV).
The surgeon continues dissection towards the proximal sigmoid colon, where the diseased tissue is located, using careful mobilization techniques.
I really like having a ICG in the ureters. It's just such a reassuring thing to be able to turn on Firefly, know where it is and do dissection without any concern of any injury.
The surgeon places a sponge to maintain exposure after completing the medial dissection and begins the lateral dissection on the descending colon, targeting a virgin tissue plane.
The surgeon reaches the previously dissected medial plane during the lateral dissection, with a visible sponge marking the area.
The surgeon clears the attachments of the mesentery from the Gerota's fascia, progressing upwards towards the flexure.
The surgeon approaches the flexure and switches to a vessel sealer for improved hemostatic control and functionality, using it to grasp and manipulate tissue.

I've been very happy with my port positions over the last couple of months and I'm able to do a lateral and superior mobilization of flexure without having to change patient position or redock. And it's become rather quick for me. So I'm just demonstrating that here.

The surgeon detaches the mesentery from the superior aspect of Gerota's fascia, progressing upward and around the area. The approach involves a lateral superior mobilization of the flexure, with occasional adjustments to a super mesocolic angle.

For those surgeons who might have a little bit of anxiety about doing a medial splenic flexor mobilization. This definitely seems to be a little bit less anxiety provoking, especially when you're seeing that natural plane, there's the pancreas there.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure using a super mesocolic approach, detaching the greater omentum from the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure. The patient remains in a steep Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated. The flexure is fully mobilized, and the surgeon removes the final attachments to the base of the pancreas using a lateral to medial approach.
With the flexure mobilized and adequate length achieved, the surgeon proceeds to take down the phlegmon, likely using surgical instruments to dissect and remove inflamed tissue.
The surgeon approaches the surgical site from an inferior angle, using a suction device in the left hand to clear smoke and assist with blunt dissection of the phlegmon.

The surgeon uses a sponge to protect the ureter while dissecting the last portion of the sigmoid colon from the sidewall, joining the medial dissection. The sponge is visibly positioned to ensure the ureter remains out of the way during the procedure.
With optimal exposure, the surgeon completes the dissection of the rectosigmoid colon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs rectal mobilization in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, aiming to reduce tension on the rectum for smoother anastomosis placement, including when using an end-to-end anastomosis (EEA) stapler.
The surgeon performs distal rectal mobilization, lifting the rectum out of the pelvis while dissecting the rectal mesentery.

This patient had not had a colonoscopy so I'm not going to do a transrectal extraction of the specimen because I would have to morselate it and I just don't do that without definitively knowing whether or not the patient had polyps or tumor.
The surgeon dissects the proximal mesentery up to the mid descending colon, avoiding high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein due to the diagnosis of diverticulitis.
The surgeon checks the length of the bowel segment and assesses its perfusion. Using cautery, the surgeon marks the area for transection, ensuring visibility even after the ICG dye washes out.
The surgeon switches to scissors to perform distal and proximal transections, preparing for a single-layer hand-sewn anastomosis after eliminating the use of EEA staplers.
I'm aware that there's really no difference in leak or anything. It's just an issue of surgeon preference. And I finally decided that I should trust my suturing ability.
The surgeon places three posterior 3-0 silk sutures to ensure the inversion of the posterior lip during the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a far lateral left-sided stitch for retraction, using two 3-0 V-Loc sutures. The surgeon's arm is visible holding up the tissue, starting posteriorly in the midline and suturing out and around the far corner.

The surgeon completes rectal mobilization, allowing the bowel to be lifted out of the pelvis for better visualization and angles. Sutures are carefully placed, transitioning from posterior to anterior, with precise bites in the corners. The surgeon parks a suture and introduces a new one through the posterior midline, continuing to suture towards the near corner.

The surgeon switches to using a canal to lay the inverted anterior and posterior lips of the anastomosis. The posterior lip is held in place by previously placed trio silk sutures, ensuring it remains inverted rather than compressed end to end.
The surgeon places each stitch precisely, bringing the current suture across to meet the originally placed suture, ensuring the junction is inverted for optimal alignment.
The surgeon ties the sutures together, completing the anastomosis with the previously placed posterior silk sutures ensuring the posterior lip remains inverted.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a 19mm proctoscope, approaching the anastomosis site. The anastomosis appears widely patent with no visible leakage.
